topics.title: Generics in Java
topics.story: Before generics (Java 1.4), a List stored Objects. You'd write list.add('hello') and then cast: String s = (String) list.get(0). If you accidentally added an Integer, the code compiled fine — but exploded at runtime with a ClassCastException. Java 5 introduced generics: List<String> tells the compiler 'this list holds only Strings'. Putting an Integer in now causes a compile-time error, not a runtime crash. But generics go further — they let you write code that works on any type while staying type-safe. A single Pair<A,B> class works for Pair<String,Integer>, Pair<LocalDate,User>, or any other combination without duplicating code.
topics.analogy: Generics are like labeled containers in a warehouse. A Box<Fragile> and a Box<HeavyMetal> are the same physical box shape, but the label tells workers what rules apply: handle with care vs use a forklift. The warehouse system (compiler) enforces those rules — you can't accidentally ship a Fragile item in a HeavyMetal box. Type erasure is like the label being removed at shipment time — the box (JVM bytecode) doesn't carry the label, but the rules were enforced during packing (compile time). Wildcards (? extends, ? super) are like saying 'any Fruit container' or 'anything a Fruit can go into'.
topics.memoryAnchor: PECS is law — Producer Extends (you READ/get from it: List<? extends Fruit>). Consumer Super (you WRITE/add to it: List<? super Apple>). Unbounded <?> = you only care it is a List, not the element type. Type erasure reality: generics are compile-time only. At runtime List<String> and List<Integer> are identical raw Lists. Cannot do at runtime: new T(), new T[], instanceof List<String>. Diamond <> lets the compiler infer: new ArrayList<>() instead of new ArrayList<String>(). Mnemonic device: I PRODUCE fruit from a bowl = extends. I CONSUME fruit into a bowl = super.
topics.firstPrinciples: Generics work via type erasure: at compile time the compiler enforces type constraints and inserts casts; at runtime the generic type parameter is erased to its bound (Object or the upper bound). So List<String> and List<Integer> are the same class at runtime (both become List). This means: you cannot do new T(), new T[]{}, instanceof T, or T.class. Wildcard ? represents an unknown type. ? extends T means 'some subtype of T' (covariant, read-safe). ? super T means 'some supertype of T' (contravariant, write-safe). Bounded wildcards enable the PECS principle. Generic methods have their own type parameter declared before the return type: <T> T identity(T x).
topics.bruteForce: Without generics: store everything as Object, cast everywhere. Object value = map.get(key); String s = (String) value; — fails at runtime if wrong type. Or copy-paste the same class for each type: IntPair, StringPair, DoublePair. Neither scales. Before generics, Java developers would write entire type-specific implementations (IntArrayList, StringHashSet) just to avoid ClassCastException.
topics.optimizedApproach: Use generic classes and methods for reusable, type-safe code. Use bounded type parameters (<T extends Comparable<T>>) to access methods of the bound. Apply PECS for wildcard methods that copy between collections. Use generic interfaces (Comparable, Iterable, Comparator) to write algorithms that work on any conforming type. Avoid raw types (List without <>) — they opt out of type safety entirely.
topics.whenToUse: Use generics whenever: writing container classes (Box<T>, Pair<A,B>, Result<T,E>), writing algorithms that work on any type (sort, search, map, filter), defining type-safe callbacks/strategies (Comparator<T>, Predicate<T>, Function<T,R>), building utility methods that preserve the type of their arguments. Avoid over-constraining — if a method only calls toString(), the parameter can be Object, not <T>. Use wildcards for API flexibility in method parameters; use concrete type parameters for class-level type safety.

examples.title: Generic Class and Method Basics
examples.tracerSteps: [{"phase":"DECLARE","line":2,"description":"Box<T>: T is a placeholder replaced at compile time"},{"phase":"CALL","line":22,"description":"Box<String> — compiler substitutes T=String everywhere in Box"},{"phase":"CALL","line":23,"description":"map() invokes own type parameter <U>: T=String, U=Integer"},{"phase":"RETURN","line":6,"description":"New Box<Integer> with mapped value 5"},{"phase":"CALL","line":30,"description":"max(String,String): T inferred as String; compareTo called"},{"phase":"RETURN","line":20,"description":"banana > apple lexicographically"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[Box T at compile time] --> B[T substituted with actual type]
    B --> C[Box String]
    B --> D[Box Integer]
    E[map Function T U] --> F[new Box U with mapped value]
    G[Pair A B] --> H[swap returns Pair B A]
    I[max T extends Comparable] --> J[a.compareTo b >= 0 ? a : b]
examples.pseudocode:
CLASS Box<T>:
  value: T
  get(): T
  map(Function<T,U>): Box<U>

CLASS Pair<A,B>:
  first: A, second: B
  swap(): Pair<B,A>
  static of(X,Y): Pair<X,Y>

STATIC max<T extends Comparable<T>>(a,b):
  return a.compareTo(b) >= 0 ? a : b
examples.explanation: Type parameters are declared in angle brackets after the class/method name. Box<T> makes T available throughout the class. The map() method has its own type parameter <U> independent of the class's T. GenericUtils.max() bounds T with Comparable<T> giving access to compareTo() inside the method. The compiler infers type arguments from the call site.
examples.realWorldUse: Java's Optional<T>, CompletableFuture<T>, Stream<T>, and ResponseEntity<T> (Spring) are generic classes. Every collection uses generics. The Box pattern appears in Result<T,E> or Either<L,R> types used in functional programming libraries like Vavr.

examples.title: Bounded Wildcards and PECS Principle
examples.tracerSteps: [{"phase":"CALL","line":5,"description":"totalCalories(apples): ? extends Fruit accepts List<Apple>"},{"phase":"LOOP_START","line":7,"description":"Each element read as Fruit — safe because Apple IS-A Fruit"},{"phase":"RETURN","line":9,"description":"2 apples x 52 calories = 104.0"},{"phase":"CALL","line":12,"description":"fillWithApples: ? super Apple accepts List<Fruit>"},{"phase":"CALL","line":13,"description":"basket.add(Apple) — safe because Fruit is supertype of Apple"},{"phase":"CALL","line":17,"description":"copy: reads src as T=Apple, writes to dst as Fruit"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[PECS] --> B[Producer Extends]
    A --> C[Consumer Super]
    B --> D[List extends Fruit]
    D -->|read as Fruit| E[Safe]
    D -->|add Fruit| F[COMPILE ERROR]
    C --> G[List super Apple]
    G -->|add Apple| H[Safe]
    G -->|read as Apple| I[COMPILE ERROR returns Object]
examples.pseudocode:
PECS:
  READ from list  use List<? extends T>  Producer
  WRITE to list   use List<? super T>    Consumer

totalCalories(List<? extends Fruit>):
  sum f.getCalories() for each f

fillWithApples(List<? super Apple>):
  basket.add(new Apple())
examples.explanation: ? extends T (upper-bounded wildcard) makes a list read-only from the type perspective. You can read elements as T but cannot add to the list because the actual element type might be a subtype. ? super T (lower-bounded wildcard) allows writing — you can add T or its subtypes but can only read as Object. PECS encodes this.
examples.realWorldUse: Collections.copy(List<? super T> dest, List<? extends T> src) uses both wildcards. Stream.of() and Comparator.comparing() use bounded wildcards. Spring's BeanFactory uses ? extends wildcards for type-safe bean retrieval.

examples.title: Generic Stack with Type Erasure Workaround
examples.tracerSteps: [{"phase":"DECLARE","line":5,"description":"Cannot create E[] due to erasure — use Object[] with unchecked casts"},{"phase":"CALL","line":11,"description":"push(1): elements[0]=1, size=1"},{"phase":"CALL","line":31,"description":"pushAll: iterates src (? extends Integer) and pushes each"},{"phase":"CALL","line":37,"description":"map(): creates new GenericStack<String>, applies mapper to each element"},{"phase":"CALL","line":35,"description":"popAll: pops elements into List<? super Integer>"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[GenericStack E] --> B[Object elements]
    B --> C[push E]
    B --> D[pop returns E]
    E[pushAll Iterable extends E] --> C
    D --> F[popAll Collection super E]
    G[map Function E R] --> H[new GenericStack R]
examples.pseudocode:
CLASS GenericStack<E>:
  elements: Object[]  erasure workaround
  push(E): elements[size++] = item
  pop():   return cast E elements[--size]
  pushAll(Iterable<? extends E>): PECS producer
  popAll(Collection<? super E>):  PECS consumer
  map(Function<E,R>): new GenericStack<R>
examples.explanation: Since Java erases generic types, you cannot create arrays of type parameters: new E[] is illegal. The workaround is Object[] with unchecked casts, suppressed with @SuppressWarnings. pushAll uses ? extends E so it accepts List<Integer> when E=Integer. popAll uses ? super E so it accepts List<Number> or List<Object>.
examples.realWorldUse: Java's ArrayList uses the same Object[] + unchecked cast pattern internally. Spring's GenericTypeResolver handles runtime generic type resolution. Guava's ImmutableList.copyOf uses ? extends E for the same PECS reason.

examples.title: Type Erasure — What Generics Cannot Do
examples.tracerSteps: [{"phase":"CALL","line":7,"description":"Both List<String> and List<Integer> erase to ArrayList at runtime"},{"phase":"RETURN","line":8,"description":"Same class object — type parameters don't exist at runtime"},{"phase":"CALL","line":14,"description":"createInstance uses Class<T> token to call constructor via reflection"},{"phase":"CALL","line":21,"description":"Heap pollution: raw list contains mixed types — no runtime check"},{"phase":"CALL","line":29,"description":"Anonymous subclass trick preserves generic type for reflection"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[List String at compile] --> B[Compiler adds casts]
    A --> C[Type erasure at runtime]
    C --> D[Just ArrayList]
    E[Cannot do] --> F[new T]
    E --> G[T.class]
    E --> H[instanceof T]
    I[Workaround] --> J[Pass Class T token]
    J --> K[clazz.newInstance]
    J --> L[clazz.isInstance obj]
examples.pseudocode:
TYPE ERASURE:
  List<String> becomes List at runtime
  List<Integer> becomes List — SAME class!

CANNOT:
  new T()      use Class<T> + reflection
  instanceof T use clazz.isInstance(obj)
  T.class      use passed Class<T> token

HEAP POLLUTION:
  raw type + cast = ClassCastException at get()
examples.explanation: Type erasure means generic type info exists only at compile time. The JVM sees List, not List<String>. You cannot create instances or arrays of type parameters. The workaround is to pass a Class<T> object as a type token. Heap pollution occurs when a raw type defeats the type system.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring's BeanFactory.getBean(Class<T>) uses type tokens. Jackson's TypeReference<List<String>>{} creates an anonymous subclass to preserve generic type info at runtime. Gson's TypeToken does the same.

examples.title: Generic Result Type — Functional Error Handling
examples.tracerSteps: [{"phase":"DECLARE","line":3,"description":"sealed Result<T,E>: only Ok and Err can implement it"},{"phase":"CALL","line":33,"description":"parsePositive('42'): parseInt succeeds, n=42 > 0 → Result.ok(42)"},{"phase":"CALL","line":41,"description":"r1.map(n->n*2): case Ok → mapper.apply(42) = 84 → Ok(84)"},{"phase":"CALL","line":42,"description":".map(n->'Result: '+n) → Ok('Result: 84')"},{"phase":"CALL","line":43,"description":"getOrElse: case Ok → returns 'Result: 84'"},{"phase":"CALL","line":44,"description":"parsePositive('abc') → Err; getOrElse → 'failed'"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
flowchart TD
    A[parsePositive s] --> B{parseInt OK?}
    B -->|yes| C{n > 0?}
    B -->|no| D[Err Not a number]
    C -->|yes| E[Ok n]
    C -->|no| F[Err Must be positive]
    E --> G[map n*2 Ok 84]
    G --> H[map Result: n]
    H --> I[getOrElse Result: 84]
    D --> J[getOrElse fallback]
examples.pseudocode:
SEALED Result<T,E>:
  Ok(value: T)
  Err(error: E)

  map(Function<T,U>):
    Ok  → Ok(mapper(value))
    Err → same Err

  flatMap(Function<T,Result<U,E>>):
    Ok  → mapper(value)
    Err → same Err
examples.explanation: Result<T,E> either holds a success value (Ok) or an error (Err). This is safer than exceptions for expected error conditions. The map() and flatMap() methods transform the value inside Ok without requiring explicit pattern matching at every call site. The sealed interface ensures exhaustive switch expressions.
examples.realWorldUse: Kotlin's Result<T>, Rust's Result<T,E>, and Java libraries like Vavr provide Either<L,R>. Spring WebFlux's Mono<T> uses similar chaining. In devlearner, a service method could return Result<User, ValidationError> instead of throwing exceptions for expected failures.

problems.title: Generic Min/Max Finder
problems.description:
Implement `<T extends Comparable<T>> T min(T[] arr)` and `max(T[] arr)` that work for any Comparable type.

**Example 1:**
min(new Integer[]{3,1,4,1,5}) => 1
max(new Integer[]{3,1,4,1,5}) => 5

**Example 2:**
min(new String[]{"banana","apple","cherry"}) => "apple"
max(new String[]{"banana","apple","cherry"}) => "cherry"
problems.hint1: T extends Comparable<T> allows calling a.compareTo(b).
problems.hint2: Iterate once: track min and max simultaneously.
problems.hint3: findKth: sort a copy, return index k-1.
problems.constraints: Single pass for findMinMax. T must implement Comparable.
problems.editorial: Iterate the list once tracking min and max with Comparator.comparingNaturalOrder() or T's compareTo(). Return a Pair<T,T>(min,max). For findKth: copy the list, sort it with Collections.sort() (requires Comparable), return index k-1. Throw IllegalArgumentException if k < 1 or k > size.

problems.title: Generic Swap and Reverse
problems.hint1: swap(arr,i,j): temp=arr[i]; arr[i]=arr[j]; arr[j]=temp.
problems.hint2: reverse: two pointers — left=0, right=n-1, swap while left < right.
problems.hint3: T[] is an array of any reference type.
problems.constraints: In-place operations. Works for any reference type array.
problems.editorial: Generic swap: T temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp. This works for any reference type T. Reverse: swap(arr, 0, arr.length-1), swap(arr, 1, arr.length-2), etc. — two-pointer approach, O(n/2) swaps. Note: you cannot create generic arrays (T[] arr = new T[n] is illegal) — pass an existing array.

problems.title: Generic Triple
problems.description:
Implement generic `Triple<A,B,C>` with three typed fields, a constructor, getters, `equals()`, `hashCode()`, `toString()`, and static factory `of(A,B,C)`.

**Example:**
Triple<String,Integer,Boolean> t = Triple.of("hello", 42, true);
t.getFirst() => "hello"
t.getSecond() => 42
t.getThird() => true
t.toString() => "(hello, 42, true)"
problems.hint1: Triple<A,B,C> has three type parameters.
problems.hint2: map1(Function<A,X> f): return new Triple<>(f.apply(first), second, third).
problems.hint3: toList(): return List.of(first, second, third).
problems.constraints: Immutable — no setters. map1 returns new Triple.
problems.editorial: Generic Triple<A,B,C> with factory of(a,b,c) for cleaner syntax. mapFirst(fn): return new Triple<>(fn.apply(first), second, third). toList(): return List.of(first,second,third) — but the list type is List<Object> since A, B, C can differ. equals/hashCode: compare all three fields.

problems.title: Generic Frequency Counter
problems.hint1: frequency: map.merge(item, 1, Integer::sum).
problems.hint2: topN: sort entries by value descending, take first n keys.
problems.hint3: Use Map.Entry and Comparator.comparingInt(Map.Entry::getValue).reversed().
problems.constraints: T must implement equals and hashCode.
problems.editorial: Use Map.merge(item, 1, Integer::sum) for clean frequency counting — one line per item. topK: entrySet().stream().sorted(Map.Entry.comparingByValue(Comparator.reverseOrder())).limit(k).collect(toList()). getMode(): same sorted stream, take first entry's key.

problems.title: Generic Binary Search Tree
problems.description:
Implement a generic `BST<T extends Comparable<T>>` with `insert(T)`, `contains(T)`, and `inOrder()` returning a sorted list.

**Example:**
BST<Integer> tree = new BST<>();
tree.insert(5); tree.insert(3); tree.insert(7); tree.insert(1);
tree.contains(3) => true
tree.inOrder() => [1,3,5,7]

**Key insight:** The `Comparable` bound lets you use `compareTo()` without knowing the concrete type.
problems.hint1: Node<T> has T value, Node<T> left, right.
problems.hint2: insert: recursively compare value.compareTo(current) to go left or right.
problems.hint3: inorder: recursive left → add root → right traversal.
problems.constraints: T extends Comparable<T>. Skip duplicates.
problems.editorial: BST insert: compare new value with root. If less, go left; if greater, go right; if equal, no duplicate (return). contains: same traversal, return true if found. inorder: left subtree → root → right subtree — produces sorted output. Size: count recursively. Height: 1 + Math.max(leftHeight, rightHeight).

problems.title: Wildcard Sum and Add
problems.description:
Use wildcards to write `double sumList(List<? extends Number> list)` that sums any list of numbers (Integer, Double, Long, etc.).

**Example 1:**
sumList(List.of(1,2,3)) => 6.0
sumList(List.of(1.5, 2.5, 3.0)) => 7.0

**Key insight:** `? extends Number` (upper bounded wildcard) allows reading from the list as `Number` — works for any subtype. Cannot add to it.
problems.hint1: ? extends Number allows reading as Number. Call number.doubleValue().
problems.hint2: ? super Integer allows adding Integer values — PECS consumer.
problems.hint3: sumList cannot add to the list — only read.
problems.constraints: PECS: sumList reads (producer extends), addToAll writes (consumer super).
problems.editorial: List<? extends Number> is an upper-bounded wildcard — you can read from it (get() returns Number) but cannot add to it (compiler rejects add()). sumList: iterate calling n.doubleValue() and accumulate. addNumbers with List<? super Integer> uses lower-bounded wildcard — you can add Integer to it.

problems.title: Generic Cache with Expiry
problems.description:
Implement `Cache<K,V>` with a TTL (time-to-live). `put(K key, V value, long ttlMs)` stores with expiry; `get(K key)` returns `Optional.empty()` if expired.

**Example:**
cache.put("token", "abc123", 1000); // expires in 1 second
cache.get("token") => Optional.of("abc123")
Thread.sleep(1100);
cache.get("token") => Optional.empty()
problems.hint1: Store Entry<V> objects containing the value and expiry time (System.currentTimeMillis()+ttl).
problems.hint2: get(): check if entry exists and currentTimeMillis() < entry.expiry.
problems.hint3: evictExpired(): removeIf on entry set where currentTime >= expiry.
problems.constraints: TTL in milliseconds. get() does lazy eviction.
problems.editorial: Store entries as CacheEntry(V value, long expiryTime = System.currentTimeMillis() + ttl). get(): check if current time < expiryTime — return null if expired and remove. evictExpired(): removeIf on entries where expiryTime <= now. size() returns current active (non-expired) count.
problems.sampleInput: put with 1000ms TTL, get before and after expiry

problems.title: Generic Graph — Adjacency Map
problems.hint1: Map<V, Map<V, E>> adjacency: outer key=vertex, inner map=neighbor to weight.
problems.hint2: addEdge: adjacency.get(from).put(to, weight).
problems.hint3: BFS: Queue<V>, Set<V> visited, standard BFS collecting vertices.
problems.constraints: Directed graph. V and E can be any types.
problems.editorial: Adjacency map: Map<V, Map<V, E>> adjacency. addEdge(from, to, weight): adjacency.get(from).put(to, weight). neighbors(v): adjacency.getOrDefault(v, Map.of()).keySet(). BFS: start from source, use Queue<V> and Set<V> visited, collect vertices in order. Dijkstra extends this with a priority queue.

problems.title: Bounded Generic Statistics
problems.description:
Implement `Statistics<T extends Number>` with `mean()`, `max()`, `min()`, `variance()` over a list of numbers.

**Example:**
Statistics<Integer> s = new Statistics<>(List.of(2,4,4,4,5,5,7,9));
s.mean() => 5.0
s.variance() => 4.0
s.max() => 9
s.min() => 2
problems.hint1: T extends Number & Comparable<T> gives both doubleValue() and compareTo().
problems.hint2: mean(): sum all doubleValue(), divide by size.
problems.hint3: median(): sort copy, middle element or average of two middles.
problems.constraints: T extends Number & Comparable<T>.
problems.editorial: T extends Number & Comparable<T>: both bounds required. mean(): sum all doubleValue() / size. min()/max(): stream().min/max(Comparator.naturalOrder()). median(): sort copy, middle element for odd size or average of two middle for even. standardDeviation(): sqrt(variance) where variance = mean of squared deviations.

problems.title: Generic Pipeline
problems.description:
Implement `Pipeline<T>` with `pipe(Function<T,T>)` and `execute(T input)`. Stages are stored as `List<Function<T,T>>` and applied in order.

**Example:**
new Pipeline<String>()
    .pipe(String::trim)
    .pipe(String::toUpperCase)
    .pipe(s -> "[" + s + "]")
    .execute("  hello  ")
=> "[HELLO]"
problems.hint1: Store the value and a List<Function> of steps.
problems.hint2: then() adds to steps list, returns this for chaining.
problems.hint3: execute() applies each function in order.
problems.constraints: Fluent API. then() returns this.
problems.editorial: Pipeline<T>: stores List<Function<T,T>> steps. then(fn) adds to list, returns this for chaining. execute(T input) applies each function in sequence using reduce or a for-loop. This is the Builder pattern plus functional composition. Similar to Stream intermediate operations but for same-type transformations.

problems.title: Type-Safe Event Bus
problems.description:
Implement a type-safe `EventBus<E>` where listeners are registered per event type using `Class<E>` as key.

**Example:**
EventBus bus = new EventBus();
bus.subscribe(UserCreated.class, e -> System.out.println("User: " + e.name()));
bus.publish(new UserCreated("Alice")) => prints "User: Alice"
bus.publish(new OrderPlaced("O1")) => no output (no subscriber)

**Key insight:** `Class<E>` token is the key — dispatches only to listeners registered for that exact event type.
problems.hint1: Map<Class<?>, List<Consumer<?>>> handlers stores type to list of consumers.
problems.hint2: publish(T event): use event.getClass() to look up handlers; cast each to Consumer<T>.
problems.hint3: subscribe: handlers.computeIfAbsent(type, k->new ArrayList<>()).add(handler).
problems.constraints: Type-safe. Multiple handlers per type allowed.
problems.editorial: Map<Class<?>, List<Consumer<?>>> handlers. subscribe(Class<T> type, Consumer<T> handler): add to handlers.get(type). publish(T event): get handlers for event.getClass(), cast each consumer to Consumer<T> (unchecked), call accept(event). The Class<T> key provides type-safe dispatch at registration time.

problems.title: Generic Merge Sort
problems.description:
Implement generic merge sort `<T extends Comparable<T>> List<T> mergeSort(List<T> list)` that works for any Comparable type.

**Example 1:**
mergeSort(List.of(3,1,4,1,5)) => [1,1,3,4,5]

**Example 2:**
mergeSort(List.of("banana","apple","cherry")) => ["apple","banana","cherry"]

**Key insight:** Generic merge sort uses `compareTo()` from the bound — one algorithm sorts integers, strings, or any Comparable.
problems.hint1: Base case: list.size() <= 1 return as-is.
problems.hint2: Split list in half, recursively sort each half, then merge.
problems.hint3: merge(): two-pointer comparing with cmp.compare(left.get(i), right.get(j)).
problems.constraints: O(n log n). Returns new list. Comparator uses ? super T.
problems.editorial: Merge sort: split list in half recursively until size 1. Merge two sorted halves with two-pointer comparison using cmp.compare(). Comparator<? super T> allows using a parent-type comparator on T — e.g., Comparator<Animal> works on List<Dog>. O(n log n) time, O(n) space.

problems.title: Generic LRU Cache
problems.description:
Implement generic `LRUCache<K,V>` with capacity limit using `LinkedHashMap` (access-order) or doubly-linked list + HashMap.

**Example:**
LRUCache<Integer,String> cache = new LRUCache<>(2);
cache.put(1,"a"); cache.put(2,"b");
cache.get(1) => "a" (1 is now most-recent)
cache.put(3,"c"); // evicts key 2 (LRU)
cache.get(2) => null
problems.hint1: Extend LinkedHashMap<K,V> with accessOrder=true, override removeEldestEntry.
problems.hint2: removeEldestEntry(eldest): return size() > capacity.
problems.hint3: Track evicted keys in removeEldestEntry.
problems.constraints: LinkedHashMap accessOrder=true. Capacity strictly enforced.
problems.editorial: Use LinkedHashMap with access-order=true: new LinkedHashMap<>(capacity, 0.75f, true). Override removeEldestEntry to return size() > capacity. get() and put() automatically move accessed entries to the tail. get() returns Optional.ofNullable(map.get(key)). Track evicted keys with removeEldestEntry.

problems.title: Generic Observable
problems.hint1: Observer<T>: void onChanged(T oldValue, T newValue).
problems.hint2: Observable<T>: List<Observer<T>>. set() saves old, updates, notifies.
problems.hint3: DerivedObservable<T,R> implements Observer<T>, stores current R value.

testCases.input: Observable<Integer> obs=new Observable<>(5); addObserver prints; obs.set(10)
DerivedObservable<Integer,String> d = new Derived(obs, i->"val:"+i); obs.set(20)

problems.constraints: Type-safe observers. DerivedObservable auto-updates.
problems.editorial: Observable<T> holds a T value and List<Observer<T>>. set(T) updates value and notifies all observers calling observer.onValueChanged(old, new). DerivedObservable<T,R> implements Observer<T>, stores current R = transform(T), notifies its own observers. This is the Reactive pattern foundation.

problems.title: Type Token Registry
problems.hint1: MultiTypeRegistry stores Map<Class<?>, TypeRegistry<?>>.
problems.hint2: getRegistry(Class<T>): return (TypeRegistry<T>) map.get(type).
problems.hint3: put/get: delegate to getRegistry(type).put/get.
problems.constraints: No casting in caller code. Each type has isolated registry.
problems.editorial: Map<Class<?>, TypedRegistry<?>> registries. getRegistry(Class<T> type): return (TypedRegistry<T>) registries.computeIfAbsent(type, k -> new TypedRegistry<>()). The unchecked cast is safe by construction — we only put TypedRegistry<T> keyed by Class<T>. This is the type-safe heterogeneous container pattern (Bloch).

problems.title: Generic Lazy Evaluation
problems.description:
Implement `Lazy<T>` that defers computation until first access:

**Example:**
Lazy<String> lazy = new Lazy<>(() -> { System.out.println("computed"); return "hello"; });
// nothing printed yet
System.out.println(lazy.get()); // => "computed" then "hello"
System.out.println(lazy.get()); // => "hello" (no recomputation)

**Key insight:** The supplier runs only once — subsequent `get()` returns the cached value.
problems.hint1: private T cachedValue; private boolean evaluated = false.
problems.hint2: get(): if (!evaluated) { cachedValue = supplier.get(); evaluated = true; } return cachedValue.
problems.hint3: map(f): return new Lazy<>(() -> f.apply(this.get())) — lazy by composition.
problems.constraints: Compute once, cache forever. map/flatMap are lazy.
problems.editorial: Lazy<T>: store Supplier<T>, boolean evaluated = false, T value = null. get(): if (!evaluated) { value = supplier.get(); evaluated = true; } return value. map(f): return new Lazy<>(() -> f.apply(this.get())) — composition defers computation. ifPresent: if supplier doesn't return null, call consumer.
problems.sampleOutput: computed once, cached on subsequent calls

problems.title: Generic Immutable List
problems.description:
Implement `ImmutableList<T>` backed by an array. Supports `get(int)`, `size()`, `add(T)` (returns new list), and `toList()`. No mutation of existing instance.

**Example:**
ImmutableList<Integer> l1 = ImmutableList.of(1,2,3);
ImmutableList<Integer> l2 = l1.add(4);
l1.size() => 3 (unchanged)
l2.size() => 4
l2.get(3) => 4
problems.hint1: private final Object[] data. All operations return new ImmutableList.
problems.hint2: prepend: new Object[size+1], copy data after index 0.
problems.hint3: map(f): iterate data, apply f, collect into new Object[].
problems.constraints: All operations return new ImmutableList. Original unchanged.
problems.editorial: Back with Object[] data. of(T... items): copy array. get(int i): (T) data[i] with unchecked cast. prepend(T item): new array of size+1, item at 0, copy rest. append: opposite. map(fn): iterate, apply fn to each, collect results into new Object[], wrap in new ImmutableList. contains: linear scan.

problems.title: Recursive Generic N-ary Tree
problems.hint1: NaryTree<T> has T value and List<NaryTree<T>> children.
problems.hint2: map(): new NaryTree<R>(f.apply(value)); add mapped children recursively.
problems.hint3: fold(identity, acc): acc.apply(identity, value), then fold each child.
problems.constraints: Recursive implementations. map() preserves tree structure.
problems.editorial: NaryTree<T>: T value, List<NaryTree<T>> children. addChild(T) creates child node and returns it for chaining. size(): 1 + sum of children sizes. depth(): 0 for no children, 1 + max child depth. stream(): flatten to Stream with Stream.concat of value stream and children's streams. fold: recursive accumulation.

problems.title: Generic Maybe Monad
problems.description:
Implement `Maybe<T>` (like `Optional`) as a generic monad with `of(T)`, `empty()`, `map(Function)`, `flatMap(Function)`, `orElse(T)`.

**Example:**
Maybe.of("hello")
    .map(String::toUpperCase)
    .map(s -> s + "!")
    .orElse("nothing")
=> "HELLO!"

Maybe.empty()
    .map(String::toUpperCase)
    .orElse("nothing")
=> "nothing"
problems.hint1: sealed interface Maybe<T> permits Present<T>, Empty<T>.
problems.hint2: map: Present → Maybe.of(f.apply(value)), Empty → empty().
problems.hint3: sequence: if any element is Empty, return empty(). Else collect all values.
problems.constraints: Java 21 sealed + records. sequence: all-or-nothing semantics.
problems.editorial: Maybe<T> wraps Optional internally or use a sealed interface. map(fn): if empty, return empty(); else return of(fn.apply(value)). flatMap(fn): if empty, return empty(); else return fn.apply(value). orElse/orElseGet: return value or default. sequence(List<Maybe<T>>): if any empty, return empty; else collect all values.

problems.title: Generic Persistent Map
problems.hint1: Store as an immutable List<Entry<K,V>> (simple linear scan is fine for learning).
problems.hint2: put(): create new list copying old entries + new entry (replacing if key exists).
problems.hint3: remove(): filter out entries with matching key, return new map.
problems.constraints: O(n) operations are fine. All operations return new map. Original never modified.
problems.editorial: Hash map with array of buckets (List<Entry<K,V>>[]). put(K,V): compute bucket = key.hashCode() % buckets.length, filter out existing key from that bucket, add new entry, return new PersistentMap with updated bucket. get(K): find entry in bucket. remove(K): filter entry out, return new map. All operations return new instances.
